1:
"And Israel abode in Shittim, and the people began to commit whoredom with the daughters of Moab." The very brief verse notes Israel's whoredom with the daughters of Moab at Shittim. The grammatical voices note the sin. Henry and Gill note that "Israel abode in Shittim, and the people began to commit whoredom with the daughters of Moab" — encamped at Shittim, the people fell into fornication with the women of Moab; the Israel abode in Shittim (the camp on the edge of the land); the the people began to commit whoredom with the daughters of Moab (the sin of fornication, the snare of Moab); the figure (the snare that the curse could not accomplish, now wrought by seduction; the truth that the enemy who could not curse Israel sought to corrupt them, the counsel of Balaam (Numbers 31:16) to draw them into sin; the figure of the danger that fellowship with the world brings, the whoredom that opened to idolatry, vv.2-3; the snare of fleshly lust, the beginning of the great sin at Peor, "they joined themselves also unto Baalpeor," Psalm 106:28).

On "Israel abode in Shittim," the witnesses note the camp. Gill notes Israel encamped at Shittim (the place of the sin) — the camp (at Shittim).

On "and the people began to commit whoredom with the daughters of Moab," the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the whoredom: "the people began to commit whoredom with the daughters of Moab" — the fornication, the snare of Moab (the counsel of Balaam, Numbers 31:16); the figure (the seduction that opened to idolatry, Psalm 106:28); the providence (the whoredom, the idolatry of Peor to follow, vv.2-3). The verse displays Israel's whoredom with the daughters of Moab.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The verse displays the sin. The verse marks Israel: at Shittim. The verse displays the whoredom: with Moab. The verse displays the snare: of seduction. The verse displays the sin: begun. The sin displays the snare that the curse could not accomplish. The verse displays the camp: at Shittim. The verse displays the whoredom: the snare. The verse displays the seduction: of Moab. Israel's whoredom with the daughters of Moab displays the snare of seduction: the people began to commit whoredom with the daughters of Moab (Numbers 31:16; Psalm 106:28) — the enemy corrupting by seduction what he could not curse, the counsel of Balaam drawing Israel into the sin that opened to idolatry. The verse displays the people joining to the idols of Moab (vv.2-3).
2:
"And they called the people unto the sacrifices of their gods: and the people did eat, and bowed down to their gods." The very brief verse notes Israel drawn to the sacrifices and worship of Moab's gods. The grammatical voices note the idolatry. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "they called the people unto the sacrifices of their gods: and the people did eat, and bowed down to their gods" — the women of Moab drew Israel to their idol-feasts, and the people ate and worshipped their gods; the they called the people unto the sacrifices of their gods (the invitation to the idol-feast); the the people did eat (eating of the sacrifices, fellowship with the idol); the and bowed down to their gods (the worship of the false gods); the figure (the whoredom leading to idolatry, the feast to the false god; the truth that fleshly sin opens the door to apostasy, the eating and bowing down a fellowship with devils, "the things which the Gentiles sacrifice, they sacrifice to devils," 1 Corinthians 10:20; the figure of the descent from lust to idolatry, the people joining themselves to the idol; the warning that fellowship with the world's feasts draws to the world's gods, 1 Corinthians 10:7-8).

On "they called the people unto the sacrifices of their gods," the witnesses note the invitation. Gill notes Israel called to the idol-feasts (the invitation) — the invitation (to the sacrifices).

On "and the people did eat, and bowed down to their gods," the grammatical-historical Ellicott and grammatical-historical note the worship: "the people did eat, and bowed down to their gods" — the eating and worship of the idol (1 Corinthians 10:20); the figure (the descent from lust to idolatry, 1 Corinthians 10:7-8); the providence (the idolatry, the joining to Baal-peor to follow, vv.3). The verse displays Israel drawn to the worship of Moab's gods.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. 3:
"And Israel joined himself unto Baalpeor: and the anger of the LORD was kindled against Israel." The witnesses note Israel joining itself to Baal-peor and the kindling of God's anger — the crux of apostasy and the wrath of God. On "Israel joined himself unto Baalpeor: and the anger of the LORD was kindled against Israel," interpreters expound the sin and its judgment. The reformed Calvin and the dominant reading note the apostasy — "Israel joined himself unto Baalpeor: and the anger of the LORD was kindled against Israel": Israel yoked itself to the idol Baal-peor, and God's anger was kindled against them; the witnesses note the significance — the apostasy of Israel, joining itself to the false god (the people, drawn by lust into idolatry, yoked to Baal-peor, the shameful idol of Moab, forsaking the living God); the lesson — the heinousness of apostasy and the certainty of God's wrath (the joining to the idol a spiritual adultery, the covenant people cleaving to the false god; "they joined themselves also unto Baalpeor, and ate the sacrifices of the dead... thus they provoked him to anger," Psalm 106:28-29); the kindling of God's anger — the holy jealousy of God against the idolatry of his people, the wrath that brought the plague (vv.9); the figure — the warning against apostasy, written for our admonition, "neither be ye idolaters... neither let us commit fornication, as some of them committed," 1 Corinthians 10:7-8; the truth that God will not share his glory with idols, his anger kindled against his people's unfaithfulness; the figure of the deadly danger of joining with the world's idols, the wrath of God against apostasy, the plague that followed the sin. The figure of the apostasy that provokes the jealous anger of God.

On "Israel joined himself unto Baalpeor," the grammatical-historical Clarke and witnesses note the joining. Clarke and Gill note Israel yoking itself to the idol Baal-peor (the apostasy, the spiritual adultery, Psalm 106:28) — the joining (to the idol).

On "and the anger of the LORD was kindled against Israel," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the wrath. The verse displays Israel joining itself to Baal-peor and the kindling of God's anger.

The verse displays the command to hang the heads of the people (vv.4-5).
4:

6:
"And, behold, one of the children of Israel came and brought unto his brethren a Midianitish woman in the sight of Moses, and in the sight of all the congregation of the children of Israel, who were weeping before the door of the tabernacle of the congregation." The very brief verse notes the brazen sin of an Israelite bringing a Midianite woman before all. The grammatical voices note the effrontery. Henry and Gill note that "one of the children of Israel came and brought unto his brethren a Midianitish woman in the sight of Moses, and in the sight of all the congregation... who were weeping before the door of the tabernacle" — even as the people wept in penitence, an Israelite (Zimri, vv.14) brazenly brought a Midianite woman in open defiance; the brought unto his brethren a Midianitish woman (the open sin); the in the sight of Moses, and in the sight of all the congregation (the brazen effrontery, in full view); the who were weeping before the door of the tabernacle (the people mourning the plague and the sin); the figure (the height of impudence, sinning openly while the people wept; the truth that sin grows bold and shameless, defying God and the congregation; the figure of the brazen wickedness that provokes God's judgment, the contempt for the holy mourning; the daring sin that called forth the zeal of Phinehas, vv.7-8).

11:
"Phinehas, the son of Eleazar, the son of Aaron the priest, hath turned my wrath away from the children of Israel, while he was zealous for my sake among them, that I consumed not the children of Israel in my jealousy." The witnesses note God's commendation of Phinehas's zeal that turned away his wrath — the crux of the zeal for God's honour reckoned and rewarded. On "Phinehas... hath turned my wrath away from the children of Israel, while he was zealous for my sake among them," interpreters expound the commendation. The reformed Calvin and the dominant reading note the zeal owned — "Phinehas... hath turned my wrath away from the children of Israel, while he was zealous for my sake among them, that I consumed not the children of Israel in my jealousy": God declared that Phinehas's zeal had turned away his wrath and spared Israel from being consumed; the witnesses note the significance — the zeal for God's honour that turned away the wrath (Phinehas, jealous for God's glory with the very jealousy of God, executed judgment and so stayed the consuming anger); the lesson — the value and reward of holy zeal for God (the zeal that is "for my sake," not of private passion but of love for God's honour, "the zeal of thine house hath eaten me up," Psalm 69:9; John 2:17); the great commendation — Phinehas's deed "was counted unto him for righteousness unto all generations for evermore," Psalm 106:30-31, an act of faith and zeal reckoned as Abraham's faith was reckoned; the figure — the holy zeal that cannot bear God's dishonour, fulfilled supremely in Christ whom the zeal of God's house consumed (John 2:17); the truth that God owns and rewards the zeal that honours him and turns away his wrath; the figure of the faithful servant whose zeal for God's glory is counted for righteousness. The figure of the zeal for God's honour that turns away wrath and is reckoned for righteousness.

On "Phinehas... hath turned my wrath away from the children of Israel," the grammatical-historical Keil and witnesses note the wrath turned. Keil and Ellicott note Phinehas turning away God's wrath, sparing Israel (the wrath stayed, the nation spared, Psalm 106:30) — the turning (the wrath away).

The verse displays God's commendation of Phinehas's zeal.

The witnesses agree the substance: Phinehas's zeal for God turned away the wrath and spared Israel (Calvin, Keil, Ellicott, Gill). The verse bears its weight as the crux of the zeal for God's honour: Phinehas's zeal — the jealousy for God's glory, answering to God's own jealousy against sin, that turned away the consuming wrath and spared the nation, an act reckoned for righteousness to all generations (Psalm 106:30-31), a figure of the holy zeal that cannot bear God's dishonour, fulfilled in Christ whom the zeal of God's house consumed (John 2:17; Psalm 69:9). The verse displays the zeal crux. God's commendation of Phinehas's zeal displays the crux: he was zealous for my sake among them, that I consumed not the children of Israel in my jealousy (Psalm 106:30-31; John 2:17; Psalm 69:9) — the zeal for God's honour that turned away the consuming wrath and spared the nation, reckoned for righteousness, a figure of the holy zeal fulfilled in Christ. The verse displays the covenant of peace given to Phinehas (vv.12-13).
12:
"Wherefore say, Behold, I give unto him my covenant of peace:" The very brief verse notes the covenant of peace given to Phinehas. The grammatical voices note the covenant. The verse holds together the gift of the covenant of peace.

The verse displays the covenant of peace given to Phinehas.

13:
"And he shall have it, and his seed after him, even the covenant of an everlasting priesthood; because he was zealous for his God, and made an atonement for the children of Israel." The witnesses note the covenant of an everlasting priesthood given to Phinehas — the crux of the priesthood secured by zeal and atonement. On "the covenant of an everlasting priesthood; because he was zealous for his God, and made an atonement for the children of Israel," interpreters expound the reward. The reformed Calvin and the dominant reading note the everlasting priesthood — "he shall have it, and his seed after him, even the covenant of an everlasting priesthood; because he was zealous for his God, and made an atonement": God gave Phinehas and his seed an everlasting priesthood, because of his zeal and the atonement he made; the witnesses note the significance — the priesthood established in Phinehas's line as the reward of his zeal (the high priesthood continued in his descendants, the covenant of a perpetual priesthood); the lesson — the reward of zeal for God, the atonement — "made an atonement for the children of Israel," the zealous act reckoned as making atonement, turning away the wrath and reconciling the people (the judgment on sin that stayed the plague counted as atonement); the figure — the priesthood that makes atonement, fulfilled supremely in Christ the great High Priest of an everlasting priesthood, "thou art a priest for ever," Hebrews 7:17, 21; Psalm 110:4, who by zeal for God's glory and by his own act made the true atonement; the figure of the everlasting priesthood and the atonement, the type of Christ the eternal Priest who made atonement for his people. The figure of the priesthood secured for ever by zeal for God and atonement made.
